narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, test scores, family background, and other details to forecast your chances. For you specifically, it calculated a 57.2% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt—high enough that the admissions team decided to reject your application to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of passing. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile that stood out the most to the model. The most influential was your LSAT score of 25, which is notably below the typical applicant's 36.7 and pushed the prediction strongly toward expecting a bar failure, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated with tougher outcomes. Next in importance was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well under the average of 5.53—which further tilted the model toward failure by signaling relatively weaker performance compared to peers at that stage. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, while slightly below the average 3.21, actually worked in your favor as the third-biggest factor, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing since it showed solid college-level promise. Other details, like your middle family income bracket and full-time student status (common among 93% of applicants), played smaller roles and didn't sway things much. Overall, your profile compared to typical students by having standout drags from the LSAT and later-year grades that outweighed your decent undergrad work. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many with similar backgrounds succeed with targeted improvements—but the model saw elevated risk based on these patterns. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it toward or above average, strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or prep, and reapplying with those updates to shift your predicted odds. Admissions teams value persistence, and addressing these areas could open doors next cycle.
